chapters.description: A study of the chemical effects of electric current, including electrolysis, electroplating, and the components of electrolytic cells.

questions.question: What is electrolysis?
questions.option_a: The process of melting metals using electricity
questions.option_b: The chemical decomposition of an electrolyte by passing an electric current through it
questions.option_c: The production of electricity using chemical reactions
questions.option_d: The flow of electrons through a metal wire
questions.hint: Focus on the 'splitting' of a compound using electrical energy.

questions.question: A substance that conducts electricity in a molten or dissolved state and undergoes chemical decomposition is called a/an:
questions.hint: Common examples include salt solutions and dilute acids.

questions.question: The electrode connected to the positive terminal of a battery is the:
questions.hint: Remember 'PANIC': Positive Anode, Negative Is Cathode.

questions.question: The electrode connected to the negative terminal of a battery is the:
questions.hint: Negative ions (anions) move away from this electrode.

questions.question: Positively charged ions that move toward the cathode during electrolysis are called:
questions.hint: Cations are attracted to the negative Cathode.

questions.question: What is electroplating?
questions.option_a: The process of cleaning metals with electricity
questions.option_b: The process of depositing a thin layer of a desired metal over another metal using electricity
questions.option_c: The process of extracting metals from their ores
questions.option_d: The process of melting two metals together
questions.hint: This is often used to prevent rusting or to make jewelry look like gold.

questions.question: During the electroplating of an iron spoon with silver, the iron spoon should be made the:
questions.hint: The object to be coated is always placed at the negative terminal.

questions.question: In the electroplating process, the electrolyte must contain:
questions.option_b: A salt of the metal to be deposited
questions.option_d: A salt of the object being coated
questions.hint: If you want to plate with copper, you need copper ions in the solution.

questions.question: Which of the following is a non-electrolyte?
questions.hint: This substance does not break into ions when dissolved in water.

questions.question: What occurs at the anode during the electrolysis of water?
questions.hint: Oxygen ions are negative, so they move to the positive electrode.

questions.question: Which statement correctly describes the movement of ions during electrolysis?
questions.option_a: Cations move to the anode and anions move to the cathode
questions.option_b: Both cations and anions move to the cathode
questions.option_c: Cations move to the cathode and anions move to the anode
questions.option_d: Ions do not move; only electrons move through the solution
questions.hint: Opposite charges attract each other.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of galvanization?
questions.option_a: To make iron look like gold
questions.option_b: To increase the conductivity of a wire
questions.option_c: To protect iron from rusting by coating it with zinc
questions.hint: This is a specific type of electroplating used for steel and iron structures.

questions.question: Which of the following will happen if you pass electricity through a copper sulfate solution using copper electrodes?
questions.option_a: Copper dissolves from the anode and deposits on the cathode
questions.option_c: Hydrogen gas is released at both electrodes
questions.option_d: The cathode dissolves and the anode gets thicker
questions.hint: This principle is used in the electrolytic refining of metals.

chapters.description: A study of the properties, extraction processes, and industrial uses of important metals including Iron, Aluminium, Copper, Silver, and Gold.

questions.question: What is steel and what is its primary use?
questions.option_a: An alloy of copper and zinc used for decoration
questions.option_b: An alloy of iron and carbon used for tools, vehicles, and appliances
questions.option_d: A mixture of non-metals used in construction
questions.hint: It is much stronger than pure iron and is the backbone of modern infrastructure.

questions.question: Which of the following is a chief ore of Iron?
questions.hint: This ore is reddish-brown and is the most important source of iron in a blast furnace.

questions.question: What is the atomic number and atomic weight of Iron (Fe)?
questions.hint: Iron is a transition metal located in the fourth period of the periodic table.

questions.question: Which metal is extracted from Bauxite ore?
questions.hint: This metal is very light and used extensively in the aircraft industry.

questions.question: Which process is used for the extraction of Aluminium?
questions.option_a: Heating with coke in a blast furnace
questions.option_b: Electrolysis of molten alumina with cryolite
questions.hint: Because this metal is highly reactive, it requires electricity for separation.

questions.question: What is the scientific name for the chief ore of Copper?
questions.hint: It is a sulfide ore that appears brassy-yellow.

questions.question: Why is copper widely used in making electric wires?
questions.option_b: It is a very good conductor of electricity
questions.hint: High electrical conductivity allows current to flow with minimal energy loss.

questions.question: How is Silver typically extracted from its ore?
questions.option_b: By froth flotation and treatment with sodium cyanide solution
questions.hint: This process involves dissolving the silver into a chemical solution before recovering it.

questions.question: Which metal is used in mirrors and for dental fillings?
questions.hint: This metal has the highest reflectivity of all metals.

questions.question: Which of the following is a physical property of Gold?
questions.option_a: It reacts quickly with air and water
questions.option_b: It is a highly malleable and ductile yellow metal
questions.option_c: It is very brittle and breaks easily
questions.option_d: It has a very low melting point
questions.hint: Gold is known for being the most hammered and stretched metal without breaking.

questions.question: In the extraction of Iron, what is the role of the Blast Furnace?
questions.option_a: To freeze the ore into blocks
questions.option_b: To reduce iron oxide to molten iron using coke and limestone at high temperatures
questions.option_c: To mix iron with copper to make an alloy
questions.option_d: To wash away impurities using high-pressure water
questions.hint: This tall, chimney-like structure uses hot air blasts to trigger chemical reactions.

questions.question: What is 'roasting' in the context of copper extraction?
questions.option_a: Heating the ore in the absence of air
questions.option_b: Heating the ore strongly in the presence of excess air to remove impurities like sulfur
questions.option_c: Cooling the metal rapidly in water
questions.option_d: Dissolving the ore in an acid
questions.hint: This process converts sulfide ores into oxides or sulfates.

questions.question: Why is cryolite (Na3AlF6) added to alumina during the extraction of Aluminium?
questions.option_a: To increase the weight of the metal
questions.option_b: To lower the melting point of alumina and increase conductivity
questions.option_c: To give the metal a shiny finish
questions.option_d: To prevent the metal from reacting with oxygen
questions.hint: Pure alumina has a very high melting point; cryolite makes the process more energy-efficient.

chapters.description: An analysis of the periodic table's history, the transition from Mendeleev’s to Moseley’s periodic laws, and the trends in atomic size and reactivity across groups and periods.

questions.question: What is the periodic table?
questions.option_a: A chart that lists all known compounds based on their boiling points
questions.option_b: A chart that organizes all known elements by atomic number and chemical properties
questions.option_c: A chart that arranges only metals according to their densities
questions.option_d: A chart that groups elements randomly without following any order
questions.hint: It serves as a fundamental framework for understanding the behavior of elements.

questions.question: On what basis did Mendeleev form his version of the periodic table?
questions.option_b: Increasing atomic masses and similar chemical properties
questions.hint: Early scientists used the weight of atoms as the primary organizing principle.

questions.question: State Mendeleev's periodic law.
questions.option_a: Properties are a periodic function of atomic numbers
questions.option_b: Properties of elements are a periodic function of their atomic masses
questions.option_c: Elements are grouped by their physical state at room temperature
questions.option_d: Elements are arranged by their discovery date
questions.hint: This law suggests that properties repeat at regular intervals when ordered by weight.

questions.question: On what basis did Moseley organize the modern periodic table?
questions.hint: He discovered that the number of protons is a more fundamental property than mass.

questions.question: What is the Modern Periodic Law?
questions.option_a: Physical and chemical properties are periodic functions of atomic masses
questions.option_b: Physical and chemical properties of elements are periodic functions of their atomic numbers
questions.option_d: Valency remains constant across a period
questions.hint: This law corrected the discrepancies found in Mendeleev's table.

questions.question: How many groups and periods are there in the Modern Periodic Table?
questions.hint: Groups are vertical columns, while periods are horizontal rows.

questions.question: What is 'Valency'?
questions.option_a: The total number of protons in an atom
questions.option_b: The combining capacity of an element
questions.option_d: The distance from the nucleus to the outer shell
questions.hint: It is determined by the number of electrons an atom loses, gains, or shares.

questions.question: As we move from top to bottom in a group, what happens to the atomic size?
questions.hint: Consider what happens when extra shells of electrons are added.

questions.question: Why does the chemical reactivity of metals increase down a group?
questions.option_a: Atomic size decreases and electrons are held tighter
questions.option_b: Atomic size increases, making it easier to lose the outermost electron
questions.option_c: The number of valence electrons increases
questions.hint: The further an electron is from the nucleus, the less attraction it feels.

questions.question: What happens to the chemical reactivity of non-metals as atomic size increases?
questions.hint: Non-metals need to attract electrons; a larger size makes the nucleus's pull weaker at the surface.

questions.question: Which of the following describes the change in valency across a period from left to right?
questions.option_a: It remains the same for all elements
questions.option_b: It increases from 1 to 4 and then decreases to 0
questions.hint: Think about the number of electrons needed to reach a stable octet.

questions.question: Why did Moseley's arrangement solve the anomalies of the Mendeleev table?
questions.option_a: Because atomic number is a more fundamental property than atomic mass
questions.option_b: Because he used more modern equipment
questions.option_c: Because he included isotopes as separate elements
questions.option_d: Because he arranged them by color
questions.hint: The identity of an element is determined by its number of protons.

chapters.description: An introduction to organic chemistry, covering the unique properties of carbon, types of hydrocarbons (alkanes, alkenes, alkynes), functional groups, and chemical reactions like combustion and addition.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.hint: Carbon is the sixth element in the periodic table.

questions.question: How many valence electrons are present in a carbon atom?
questions.hint: These are the electrons in the outermost shell that participate in bonding.

questions.question: Which type of chemical bond does carbon mainly form?
questions.hint: Carbon usually shares electrons rather than transferring them completely.

questions.question: What is the general formula for Alkanes?
questions.hint: Alkanes are saturated hydrocarbons where every carbon is bonded to the maximum number of hydrogens.

questions.question: Which of the following is a saturated hydrocarbon?
questions.hint: Saturated hydrocarbons contain only single bonds between carbon atoms.

questions.question: What is the simplest organic compound?
questions.hint: It consists of one carbon atom and four hydrogen atoms.

questions.question: Which functional group is characteristic of alcohols?
questions.hint: This is known as the hydroxyl group.

questions.question: The property by which a large number of carbon atoms link with each other to form long chains is called:
questions.hint: This self-linking ability allows for the existence of millions of organic compounds.

questions.question: What happens when an unsaturated hydrocarbon undergoes an 'addition reaction' with hydrogen?
questions.option_d: It decomposes into carbon and hydrogen
questions.hint: The double or triple bonds break to accommodate the new hydrogen atoms.

questions.question: A hydrocarbon with the molecular formula C3H6 belongs to which category?
questions.hint: Compare the formula to the general formula CnH2n.

questions.question: Compounds having the same molecular formula but different structural arrangements are known as:
questions.hint: Think of Butane and Iso-butane; they have the same atoms but different shapes.

questions.question: What is the product formed when ethanol is oxidized?
questions.hint: The -OH group is converted into a -COOH group.

questions.question: Which of the following will decolorize bromine water?
questions.hint: This is a common test to distinguish between saturated and unsaturated hydrocarbons.

chapters.description: A comprehensive look at the classification of materials based on their properties and origins, including natural and synthetic fibers, biodegradable and non-biodegradable substances, and the industrial application of metals, plastics, and glass.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a natural fiber?
questions.hint: These fibers are obtained directly from plants or animals.

questions.question: Which material is most commonly used for making electric wires due to its high conductivity?
questions.hint: It is a reddish-brown metal known for being an excellent conductor of electricity.

questions.question: What is a characteristic property of synthetic fibers like Nylon?
questions.option_a: They are easily decomposed by bacteria
questions.option_b: They are made from plant cellulose
questions.option_c: They are strong, durable, and human-made
questions.option_d: They absorb a lot of water and dry slowly
questions.hint: These fibers are manufactured in industries using chemical processes.

questions.question: Which of the following is a biodegradable material?
questions.hint: Biodegradable materials can be broken down naturally by microorganisms.

questions.question: Why is rubber used as an insulation coating for electric wires?
questions.option_a: It is a good conductor of heat
questions.option_b: It is a non-conductor of electricity (insulator)
questions.option_d: It helps the current flow faster
questions.hint: Insulators prevent electric current from leaking or causing shocks.

questions.question: Which material is best suited for making water tanks because it is lightweight, durable, and resistant to corrosion?
questions.hint: This material is non-reactive with water and easy to mold into large shapes.

questions.question: What is the primary environmental concern regarding non-biodegradable materials like plastic?
questions.option_b: They provide nutrients to the soil
questions.option_c: They persist in the environment for a long time and cause pollution
questions.option_d: They are too expensive to produce
questions.hint: These materials do not break down naturally, leading to waste accumulation.

questions.question: Why is Aluminum preferred over Iron for making many cooking utensils?
questions.option_b: It is a better conductor of heat and does not rust easily
questions.option_c: It is a very good insulator
questions.option_d: It is the cheapest metal available
questions.hint: Efficient heat transfer and durability are key for kitchenware.

questions.question: Which material would be most suitable for making a raincoat and why?
questions.hint: A raincoat needs to repel water rather than absorb it.

questions.question: What happens to a non-biodegradable substance if it is buried in the soil for several years?
questions.option_d: It increases the fertility of the soil
questions.hint: Microorganisms in the soil cannot digest these synthetic substances.

questions.question: Which material is used for making windows because it is transparent and hard?
questions.hint: This material allows light to pass through while providing a solid barrier.

questions.question: Ceramics are often used for making tea cups and electric insulators because they:
questions.option_b: Can withstand high temperatures and do not conduct electricity
questions.hint: These materials are brittle but have excellent thermal and electrical resistance.

questions.question: If you are designing a tyre that needs flexibility and good grip, which material is most appropriate?
questions.hint: This material has high friction and elasticity.
